BMW X5 M50i / M60i Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 M50i or M60i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned N63 engine for the M50i typically costs £9,000–£13,000 supply only. A reconditioned S68 for the M60i costs £10,000–£17,000. Used units are available from £2,000–£6,500. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 M50i / M60i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N63 in the M50i has a notorious reputation — excessive oil consumption, timing chain stretch, turbocharger failures, and valve stem seal leaks are common. The S68 in the M60i is an all-new engine that replaced the N63. It incorporates a 48V mild-hybrid system and improved cooling, but retains the hot-vee design and is largely unproven. Both engines demand diligent maintenance — especially frequent oil changes (3,500–5,000 miles).

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 M50i and M60i?

acceptedAnswer.text: M50i (N63): timing chain stretch and guide failure, excessive oil consumption and valve stem seal leaks, turbocharger failure, coolant leaks. M60i (S68): unproven long-term reliability — early reports include battery failure, drivetrain malfunction warnings, and software issues. The hot-vee design is a common factor in both engines.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 M50i / M60i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: X5 M50i and M60i values remain strong — a well-kept example is often worth £30,000–£60,000+. A £9,000–£17,000 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — within the 70% BMW threshold, but a significant investment. Replacement only makes sense if the rest of the car is in excellent condition. For M60i owners, warranty coverage is essential as the S68 is unproven.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 M50i or M60i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. G05 X5 M50i (2019–2023) uses N63B44T3; G05 X5 M60i (2023+) uses S68B44A. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N63 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It needs replacement — the chain guides break and the tensioner wears. A full timing chain, tensioner, guide and VANOS sprocket replacement costs £3,000–£6,000. It's a labour-intensive job but far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£9,000+). At the first sign of rattling from the front of the engine on an N63, book an inspection immediately. For the S68, long-term timing chain data is not yet available.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW X5 M50i / M60i Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current X5 M50i/M60i market. N63-engined cars sit at the lower end; S68 engines command a premium. A replacement N63 motor can cost upwards of £12,000, while S68 units are even more expensive. See the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X5 M50i/M60i V8 engine typically takes 16–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The hot-vee design makes many repairs exceptionally labour-intensive.

problems.title: N63 Timing Chain Stretch and Guide Failure (M50i)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 2–5 seconds, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors, eventual chain slip
problems.repairCost: £3,000–£6,000 (timing chain, tensioner, guides and VANOS sprocket replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £9,000–£13,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any rattling from the front of the engine on an N63 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N63's timing chain guides are made of brittle plastic that can break apart, sending metal fragments through the engine. The "hot-vee" design generates extreme heat loads that accelerate component wear. BMW faced widespread complaints over N63 timing chain and oil consumption issues. If you hear any rattling from the front of the engine, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement. Frequent oil changes (every 3,500–5,000 miles) are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: N63 Excessive Oil Consumption and Valve Stem Seal Leaks (M50i)
problems.symptoms: Oil level dropping between services, blue smoke on cold start or after idling, low oil pressure warning, puff of smoke when accelerating

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: the N63 can burn significant amounts of oil between services

problems.recommendation: The N63 is notorious for excessive oil consumption. This is caused by valve stem seal leaks, turbocharger oil seal failure, and PCV system issues. BMW even launched a Customer Care Package program to address widespread issues. Check oil levels regularly — if you're topping up more than 1L between services, have the valve stem seals and turbochargers inspected. Ignoring oil consumption can lead to catastrophic engine failure. Extended oil change intervals cause coking issues in the hot-vee engine — many specialists recommend oil changes every 3,500–5,000 miles instead.

failureLink.label: Read the full valve stem seal failure guide

problems.title: S68 Unproven Reliability and 48V System Concerns (M60i)
problems.symptoms: Limited long-term data — early reports include battery failure, drivetrain malfunction warnings, and software issues
problems.typicalMileage: Unknown — early reports at low mileage (6,200 miles)
problems.repairCost: Unknown — S68 is a new engine; 48V system repairs could be costly

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: long-term reliability of the S68 is unproven

problems.recommendation: The S68 is an all-new engine that replaced the N63 in the 2023 X5 LCI. While it benefits from updates including 48V mild-hybrid technology, improved oil cooling and revised manifolds, it retains the hot-vee design and is largely unproven in the long term. Early reports include battery failure and drivetrain malfunction warnings. The 48V system adds complexity and potential failure points. If you're buying an M60i, ensure it's under warranty and consider an extended warranty. Only time will tell if the S68 is more reliable than its predecessor.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: X5 M50i / M60i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Specialist job — far cheaper than a new engine
Turbo replacement — catch before debris damages the engine

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X5 M50i / M60i? Check These First
buyIf: * M60i with full warranty coverage — the S68 is unproven and warranty is essential
* M50i with documented timing chain work — evidence of the upgrade is essential
* Full service history with frequent oil changes (3,500–5,000 mile intervals — not BMW's extended schedule)
* A well-maintained example where the previous owner understood the N63's needs
avoidIf: * M50i with no proof of timing chain work — you're buying a known risk
* Any rattling from the front of the engine — timing chain issues are likely
* Blue smoke on cold start — valve stem seals are failing
* Poor service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Any engine warning light without a clear diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X5 M50i and M60i are genuine performance SUVs — 530 hp, 750 Nm, 0–62 mph in 4.3 seconds. The N63 that powers the M50i is a masterpiece of engineering with a notorious reputation — timing chain issues, oil consumption, and turbocharger failures are well-documented. The S68 in the M60i is a significant step forward, but it's still unproven. Buy on history, maintain religiously (oil changes every 3,500–5,000 miles), and keep it under warranty. Skip the history and it will punish you.

cta.label: → Compare M50i / M60i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 M50i or M60i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattling from the front of the engine? (N63 timing chain warning — a critical check)
2. Blue Smoke — Any blue smoke on cold start or after idling? (N63 valve stem seal warning)
3. Service History — including which generation (M50i or M60i) and evidence of frequent oil changes
4. Oil Consumption — How much oil does it use between services? (N63s are known for oil thirst)
5. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (cooling system warning on both engines)
6. Turbo Performance — Test acceleration; any whistling, hesitation, or loss of power?
7. Warranty Status — Is the car under warranty? (Essential for M60i S68 ownership)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
